figures.caption: Table 5 Hyperparameters for the RGB-XYZ Model.
Figure 23 YAM real-world tasks and setup.

Figure 4 Qualitative cross-embodiment rollouts. PointAction executes on two real arms unseen during 4D-video pretraining: YAM (top) and xArm7 (bottom), each on a different task. Each RGB frame is annotated (top right) with the segmented robot points predicted by the 4D-VM.

Table 2 Cross-embodiment real-world evaluation on two arms unseen during 4D-video pretraining. (a) xArm7: 100 rollouts per task with randomized initial object poses; all methods finetuned on 50 expert trajectories per task; compared against the most recent VLAs (π0.5, GR00T N1.7). (b) YAM arm: 20 evaluations per task; all methods finetuned on 20 expert trajectories per task; compared against π0 and GR00T N1.5. PointAction’s pointbased interface transfers cleanly to both arms, substantially outperforming VLA baselines on every task and arm.

Figure 24 Qualitative 4D generation by PointAction on real-world rollouts. Top: a held-out trajectory from DROID [28]; bottom: a held-out trajectory from BridgeData V2 [53]. Columns: four uniformly sampled timesteps. Rows: (a) generated RGB frames, (b) generated XYZ pointmaps, (c) rendered XYZ as a 3D point cloud.

Figure 25 Qualitative comparisons on RGB and depth generation. Top: DROID [28]. Bottom: BridgeData V2 [53]. PointAction (rightmost column group) produces sharper RGB and cleaner pixel-aligned depth than baselines.

Table 3 Ablation on action decoder inputs. We jointly ablate (i) the input modality fed to the decoder (RGB pixels vs. XYZ pointmaps) and (ii) the point source used for the XYZ stream (full-scene vs. robot+scene vs. robotonly). Robot-only XYZ points yield the best ID and OOD-Env performance, validating both the choice of geometric representation and the robot-centric masking. Each configuration is evaluated 100 times with randomized initial states.
Figure 2 Overview of the universal video-to-point model. We fine-tune a pretrained video model to jointly predict RGB rollouts and dynamic 3D pointmaps using the Diffusion Forcing objective.
Figure 5 Visualization of simulation evaluation settings.

Table 6 Per-task success rates (%) on RoboCasa365 across the three ID/OOD regimes. Top block: 10 training tasks evaluated in In-Distribution (ID) and Out-of-Distribution Environment (OOD-Env) regimes; each cell reports ID |OOD-Env. Bottom block: 5 completely unseen tasks evaluated in the OOD-Task regime (zero-shot instruction following); each cell reports a single OOD-Task success rate. Every (method, task, regime) cell is computed from 100 rollouts with randomized initial state. Bold marks the best result per row.

Figure 3 Overview of the point-to-action decoder. Given the predicted robot-centric pointmaps, we downsample each frame via Farthest Point Sampling and encode it with a lightweight PointNet-style MLP. A thin conditional DiT decoder then denoises the full action sequence in parallel, taking the per-frame point features as token-aligned conditioning and injecting the diffusion timestep and the initial robot state via AdaLN.
